ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general oyster food product class; steamed preparation variant is not separately coded. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LQ covers oysters as a food commodity; steamed preparation is not separately enumerated. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888001 covers oyster as a food substance; steamed preparation variant is not separately coded. Closest parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of steamed oysters is approximately 6 medium deshelled oysters, weighing around 90 g, commonly served as a starter or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Oysters, steamed — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F03050029 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15169 (oysters, eastern, cooked, moist heat) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown.

gi_source.title: N/A — Steamed oyster; glycemic index is not applicable as shellfish glycogen carbohydrate does not produce a measurable glycaemic response
gi_source.source_match_notes: Steamed oyster contains 9.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g, primarily shellfish glycogen, which has negligible glycaemic impact and is not classified in the University of Sydney GI Database. No peer-reviewed GI value exists for oysters. gi_category is set to not_applicable and glycemic_index is set to 0 (numeric sentinel per template requirement) to indicate no measurable glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving is set to 0.0 accordingly.

hypertension.key_factor: Favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (212 mg Na : 302 mg K per 100 g) aligned with DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Steamed oysters without added condiments are suitable for hypertensive individuals; patients should avoid adding soy sauce, oyster sauce, or other high-sodium condiments, which would substantially increase the sodium load per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High protein (18.9 g/100 g), zero sugar, and shellfish glycogen carbohydrate with negligible glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed oysters are appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their high protein content, absence of added sugar, and shellfish glycogen carbohydrate that does not meaningfully raise postprandial blood glucose; patients should avoid sweet dipping sauces or glazes that add free sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 100 mg/100 g is moderate; however, omega-3 fatty acids and high iron content provide cardioprotective benefits
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed oysters provide omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (~1.5 g/100 g) and a low saturated fat content (1.3 g/100 g) that support cardiovascular health, but individuals with established hypercholesterolaemia should moderate portion size to 6–8 oysters per serving to manage dietary cholesterol intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High iron (9.2 mg/100 g), omega-3 fatty acids, and zinc (oysters are the richest dietary zinc source) support immune regulation and reduce joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Steamed oysters are beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to their anti-inflammatory omega-3 content and immune-supporting micronutrients; patients should ensure oysters are thoroughly cooked to prevent foodborne illness that could trigger RA flares, and should avoid high-sodium condiments that may worsen tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed oyster is a minimally processed seafood dish consisting of fresh marine bivalve mollusks (Crassostrea spp. or similar species) that have been steamed until cooked through and served deshelled. The flesh is tender, plump, and soft in texture with a naturally briny, mildly sweet oceanic flavour. Steaming preserves the oyster's natural juices and nutritional integrity without the addition of fats or oils. Per 100 g, steamed oysters provide 163 kcal, 18.9 g protein, 4.6 g fat (of which approximately 1.3 g is saturated and 1.5 g polyunsaturated including omega-3 fatty acids), and 9.9 g carbohydrate primarily as shellfish glycogen with negligible glycaemic impact. They are an exceptional source of iron (9.2 mg/100 g, exceeding 50% of adult daily requirements) and a well-known dietary source of zinc. Sodium is moderate at 212 mg/100 g and potassium is 302 mg/100 g, yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio aligned with DASH dietary principles. Cholesterol is 100 mg/100 g. Steamed oysters are suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and rheumatoid arthritis, and are appropriate in cardiovascular diets when consumed in moderate portions. They are gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. Common serving contexts include appetisers, seafood platters, and high-protein meal components.
common_user_queries: Is steamed oyster good for diabetes?
How many calories in steamed oysters?
Is steamed oyster high in sodium?
Can I eat oysters if I have high blood pressure?
Are steamed oysters high in iron?
Is oyster good for rheumatoid arthritis?
What is the protein content of steamed oysters?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient data sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F03050029, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15169 (oysters, eastern, cooked, moist heat), an approved secondary source. All core macros are consistent with known oyster nutritional profiles.
notes: Vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for steamed oyster are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F03050029. USDA FoodData Central NDB 15169 reports zinc at approximately 59 mg/100 g (cooked eastern oyster) and vitamin B12 at approximately 16 mcg/100 g, but these values are for eastern oyster (Crassostrea virginica) and may not directly correspond to the species represented in the HPB record; therefore these fields are conservatively set to -1 pending species-specific confirmation. Trans fat is set to 0.0 as oysters are a marine invertebrate with no industrially produced trans fats; any naturally occurring trans fat is below detection threshold (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). Sugar_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source — oysters contain no plant-based dietary fibre). glycemic_index is set to 0 (numeric value per template requirement) with gi_category not_applicable: steamed oyster contains shellfish glycogen carbohydrate (9.9 g/100 g) that does not produce a measurable glycaemic response; no GI value for oysters exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per serving is set to 0.0 accordingly. Ontology codes are approximate matches as steamed preparation variants are not separately enumerated in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Steamed Oyster', a precise canonical English food name. This is a generic international seafood item, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish requiring mandatory local-language names under G6. G6 multilingual compliance is satisfied as no mandatory non-English language applies to this food category.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (163.0), protein_g (18.9), fat_g (4.6), carbohydrate_g (9.9) all present and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo F03050029 (reviewed, confidence 0.95). nutrient_source fully documented.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (212.0), saturated_fat_g (1.3), sugar_g (0.0) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible and supported by HPB and USDA FoodData Central respectively.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is set to numeric value 0 (satisfying the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric), gi_category is set to not_applicable, glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0, and gi_source is fully documented explaining that no GI value exists for oysters in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature and that shellfish glycogen does not produce a measurable glycaemic response.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03302757 (oyster food product), FoodEx2 A00LQ (Oysters), and SNOMED CT 226888001 (Oyster food) are all defensible approximate matches. All are documented as 'closest_parent' with gap notes explaining that steamed preparation variants are not separately coded. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, nutritional profile, clinical relevance, and serving context. Suitable for RAG and LLM training use.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, human_condition_name correctly set), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis also included.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 8 English-language variants including alternate names, preparation descriptors, and common search forms. aliases_by_language english array mirrors all flat alias entries. All strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. No mandatory non-English aliases required for this generic international food category.
